intent:
The user's own reproduction, which no earlier scenario covers:

  1. open a library with many entries
  2. ZOOM OUT until the screen is full of 'loading' tiles and the thumb
     workers stack up
  3. click a movie and open quickview / fullview — "movie will not play,
     in some cases the application locks up and needs to be killed"

The zoom-out is the load-bearing step and the reason this differs from
`slow_disk_watch_under_sweep` (03/04). Zooming out multiplies the VISIBLE
tile count, and every visible tile pushes a tier-1 `Thumb` request. Tier 1
sits at the TOP of the strict-priority ladder in `Queues::pop`, and — unlike
the `gen` sweep — it is subject to NO live throttle: `gen_live_cap` and the
anim-in-flight pause both gate `gen` only. So a zoom-out converts a
throttled background sweep into an unthrottled foreground flood that holds
every worker for as long as the visible set stays unfilled.

What to read, in order:
  - `queue_wait: thumb` (new) — the scheduler-latency instrument. Job
    duration was already measured; this is how long a request SITS. If the
    visible thumbs the user is waiting on show multi-second/minute waits
    while `worker_utilisation` is ~1.0, the pool is not stalled, it is
    saturated by work of its own top tier, and the ladder is the bug.
  - `thumb_requests` vs `library_count` — sizes the flood the zoom-out
    admits. Compare zoomed-in (control) against zoomed-out.
  - `decode reads` (new) — the VIDEO thread. `av_read_frame` wall time on
    the live lane's reader thread. This is the direct measure of "movie
    will not play": a reader blocked on a contended SMB link produces no
    frames while the app's tick times stay clean. `>100ms` / `>1s` are the
    counts that matter; the mean stays microseconds even in a bad run.
  - `selected_served` — time from pressing space to the FIRST frame, with
    the storm already loose. Against `cold_open_quickview` (no contention)
    this is the user-visible "won't play" number.
  - served-frame gaps + `reanchors` — whether playback, once started,
    survives the storm.
  - `render_stall_*` — review 04's finding (render-thread fs on the source
    volume); included so a lockup can be attributed to the UI thread vs
    the video thread rather than guessed at.

Point $SB_BENCH_LIBRARY at the corpus. Sweep with
`--set gen_live_concurrency=0` to confirm the gen throttle is NOT what is
holding things (it gates the wrong tier for this symptom).
